title: Multi-Persona Simulations
concept: Prompting a model to simulate a debate or panel of experts within a single response.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: In the last lesson, how did we control how DEEP or technical an explanation should be?

options.text: By specifying the audience, like 'explain to a beginner' or 'explain to an engineer'
By asking the AI to use only short words
By repeating the question three times

exercises.explain: Right! Naming the audience (a beginner, an executive, a fellow engineer) tells the model how much background detail and jargon to include. Now we'll build on that by having the AI juggle MULTIPLE personas in one response.

cards.title: One Brain, Many Voices
cards.body: What if you could put three experts in a room, have them argue it out, and read the whole debate in seconds — without ever leaving your chat window? You can, with one clever prompt.

cards.title: What is Multi-Persona Simulation?
cards.body: It's a prompting technique where you ask one AI to 'play' several characters or experts at once, having them respond, disagree, or build on each other — all inside a single answer.

cards.title: Like a talk show with three guests
cards.body: Imagine one actor playing a host, a scientist, and a skeptic on a talk show, switching voices for each. That's what the model does when you ask for a panel discussion in one response.

cards.title: Try This Prompt
cards.body: "Simulate a conversation between a nutritionist, a fitness coach, and a busy parent discussing whether kids should eat breakfast before school. Give each person 2-3 lines and let them respond to each other."

cards.title: Myth: You need multiple AI accounts
cards.body: You don't need to open several chats or use different tools to get different viewpoints. One single prompt to one model can generate an entire labeled panel discussion, because the model can hold multiple personas in one response.

cards.title: Single Voice vs. Panel
cards.body: Compare asking for one expert opinion versus asking for a simulated group discussion with multiple viewpoints bouncing off each other.

left.text: "Act as a lawyer and explain contract law." → One consistent viewpoint, one voice throughout.

right.text: "Simulate a lawyer, a client, and a judge discussing a contract dispute." → Multiple voices, disagreement, and back-and-forth in one response.

exercises.prompt: Which prompt is the BEST example of asking for a multi-persona simulation?

options.text: Act as a scientist and explain climate change to a beginner.
Have a scientist, a farmer, and a politician debate the best response to climate change, with each giving their perspective in turn.
Summarize the causes of climate change.

exercises.explain: Option C asks for three distinct roles interacting with each other in one response — that's the core of multi-persona simulation. Option B is just single-persona role prompting (one voice), not a panel or debate.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To simulate a panel discussion, you must send separate prompts to the AI for each persona and then manually combine their answers.
exercises.explain: False — a single well-written prompt can ask the model to simulate all personas at once, having them respond to each other within one continuous output. You don't need separate prompts or manual copy-pasting.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete this multi-persona prompt.
exercises.sentence: Simulate a debate between a doctor and a chef about sugar, and label each speaker so I can tell who is talking.
exercises.explain: The word 'label' is key — it tells the model to clearly mark which persona is speaking at each turn, so the panel discussion stays organized and easy to follow instead of blending into one voice.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three panel-discussion outputs was most likely generated by an AI simulating personas (versus a real recorded transcript)?

options.text: A transcript with natural interruptions, filler words like 'um', crosstalk, and inconsistent turn lengths typical of a live recording.
A neatly formatted exchange where each of three 'experts' speaks in perfectly balanced paragraphs, never interrupts, and always politely agrees to disagree.
A messy chat log with typos, one person dominating the conversation, and someone going off-topic.

exercises.explain: AI-simulated debates tend to be suspiciously tidy — equal turn lengths, polished grammar, and overly balanced, polite disagreement. Real human debates are messier, with interruptions, uneven speaking time, and natural imperfections.

exercises.prompt: You want an AI to help you understand whether homework should be banned, by showing multiple sides fairly. Which prompt best sets up a multi-persona simulation?

options.text: Tell me if homework should be banned or not.
Simulate a debate between a teacher who supports homework, a student who opposes it, and a psychologist who weighs in on both views. Label each speaker.
Write a persuasive essay against homework.
Explain homework policy like I'm 5 years old.

exercises.explain: Option B names specific, distinct personas (teacher, student, psychologist), gives each a clear stance or role, and asks for labeled turns — this is exactly what triggers a multi-persona simulation instead of a single flat answer. The other options ask for one voice or one opinion, not a panel.
